behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: Testing this requires both git's rename detection and the parser's categorization logic. A mocked git diff might include a rename marker, but only a real test repository with actual git rename operations verifies that git is invoked with rename detection enabled (e.g., -M flag) and that the output is correctly parsed. The failure mode is: tool counts renames as delete+add instead of single rename due to either parser error or git misconfiguration.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary git repository with tracked files, perform a rename (git mv), commit, run the tool, and verify: (1) exactly one file appears in the 'renamed' category, (2) the file does NOT appear in both 'deleted' and 'added' categories, (3) total change count is correct (one rename, not two operations).

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: This is a robustness requirement: the parser must handle git's binary file markers without crashing and still count the files. A mocked git diff showing 'Binary files ... differ' markers can test parser logic (Unit), but a real test repository with actual binary files (images, compiled artifacts) ensures git produces the expected output and the tool handles it end-to-end. The failure mode is parser crash or silent omission of binary files from counts.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary git repository with binary files (e.g., .png, .zip, .jar), make changes (add, modify, delete binary files), run the tool, and verify: (1) tool completes without crashing, (2) binary files are included in added/modified/deleted counts, (3) output format is valid JSON/structured format, (4) binary files are not parsed for content (no attempted diff display).

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: This tests error handling when git has no history to report. A mocked git that returns an empty result (Unit) can verify the parser handles it, but a real empty repository (Integration) ensures the tool invokes git correctly on this edge case and handles any error codes gracefully. The failure mode is: tool crashes, hangs, or outputs malformed report when run on fresh repo.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a temporary git repository without any commits (git init, no git add/commit), run the tool, and verify: (1) tool exits successfully with no error code, (2) output is valid (empty list or empty report, not null/error), (3) output format matches schema even when empty, (4) tool does not hang or timeout waiting for commits.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: This is the core correctness guarantee. Testing requires a real repository with known, deterministic changes to verify the tool's counts are accurate. A mocked git output with predefined counts (Unit) tests the parser but risks missing git invocation bugs or edge cases. Integration with a real test repository directly verifies the invariant: tool output must match actual repo state. Failure mode: off-by-one errors, miscounting, or silently dropping changes.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary git repository with a controlled sequence of changes: add N files, delete M files, rename K files, modify J files across multiple commits. Run the tool and verify: (1) added count equals N, (2) deleted count equals M, (3) renamed count equals K, (4) modified count equals J, (5) sum of all counts matches total unique files affected, (6) no double-counting or omissions. Repeat with different N, M, K, J values.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: This tests whether the tool correctly reconciles staged and unstaged changes in the index. A mocked git with separate 'staged' and 'unstaged' outputs (Unit) can verify the merging logic, but only a real repository with actual staged/unstaged state verifies that git is invoked correctly and that the tool treats both states identically. Failure mode: tool reports different counts or files depending on git index state, leading to CI/dev environment inconsistencies.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary git repository with tracked files and staged/unstaged changes. Test both scenarios: (A) make changes and stage them (git add), (B) make identical changes but do NOT stage them. For each scenario, run the tool and collect output. Verify: (1) output from A and B are bit-for-bit identical, (2) same files appear in added/modified/deleted categories regardless of staging, (3) counts are identical between staged and unstaged, (4) tool does not raise environment/index state warnings.
